EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved out of the southern high plains toward eastern Wisconsin. Heavy snow and strong winds tracked from southwest and west central Minnesota into west central Wisconsin. There was a wide swath of 6 to 10 inches of snow, most of which fell prior to 1600 CST. During the afternoon of the 23rd and into the early morning hours of the 24th, winds gusted around 35 mph creating near-blizzard conditions, chiefly in open country of western and southern Minnesota.  Many counties recommended no travel, and numerous schools were closed. Several hundred accidents occurred, especially around the Minneapolis-St. Paul metropolitan area.  The top snowfall total, 10 inches, fell at Canby, Long Prairie, Little Falls, and Onamia.  Eight inches were registered at Dawson, Benson, Willmar, Buffalo and Milaca. Six inches were reported as far south as St. James, Mankato, and Savage.

Heavy snow fell across northeastern Minnesota. Light snow started Saturday afternoon, the 22nd, and accumulated a couple inches, then the heaviest snow fell Sunday, the 23rd. The heaviest snow band of over 10 inches was in a 60 mile-wide band centered on a Brainerd to Crane Lake line. Some specific amounts include 15 inches at both Lake Kabetogama and Crane Lake and 13 inches at Brainerd and Coleraine.  Duluth had 9 inches.

A wide swath of 6 to 10 inches, with a few pockets of 11 and 12 inches, swept from southwest Minnesota, across the Minneapolis-St. Paul area, into west central Wisconsin.  Winds across this area were 25 to 30 mph, with blowing and drifting snow in open areas.  Although some parts of far south central Minnesota only picked up 4 to 6 inches, winds in this area were a little stronger, creating near-blizzard conditions. The greatest snowfall totals were in the Twin Cities metro, where Chaska, Chanhassen and New Hope all picked up 11 inches. Ten inches were recorded at Lamberton, Springfield and Gaylord. There was a sharp cutoff on the northern edge of the snow; e.g. Lamberton in southernmost Redwood County, tallied 10 inches, while Belview, 25 miles to the north in far northern Redwood County, only saw 2 inches. Rockford, straddling the Hennepin/Wright County line, received 6 inches, whereas Buffalo, 10 miles to the northwest in central Wright County, noted only 1 inch.

A very narrow band of snow, only about 15 miles wide, developed across west central Minnesota amid rapid mid-level frontogenesis.  Snowfall rates were one to two inches per hour as the band of snow remained stationary during late afternoon and evening. Madison picked up 10.5 inches, Dawson and Watson received 7 inches, as did the Regional Treatment Center on the north side of Willmar.  Areas only 10 to 15 miles north or south of this snow band only picked up an inch or two of snow (e.g. Montevideo, just 7 miles southeast of Watson, received 2 inches, and New London, just 10 miles north of the Willmar Treatment Center, only picked up 2 inches).  The band of snow weakened substantially as it moved east of Willmar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A MNDOT Road Weather System measured 65 mph (56 knots) along Interstate 90 near Blue Earth in Faribault County, while the Fairmont Airport in Martin County measured a 51 knot gust at 1235 CST. The strongest sustained wind at Fairmont was 37 knots. The high wind occurred in far southern Minnesota due to low pressure and a cold front moving from South Dakota into Wisconsin, with high pressure moving in behind the low.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: 70 foot HAM radio tower toppled.
Tree down on camper and vehicle. Three children playing inside the camper and three adults standing outside the camper were not injured.
Hail pounded houses, vehicles and crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very narrow band of snow, only about 15 miles wide, developed across west central Minnesota amid rapid mid-level frontogenesis.  Snowfall rates were one to two inches per hour as the band of snow remained stationary during late afternoon and evening. Madison picked up 10.5 inches, Dawson and Watson received 7 inches, as did the Regional Treatment Center on the north side of Willmar.  Areas only 10 to 15 miles north or south of this snow band only picked up an inch or two of snow (e.g. Montevideo, just 7 miles southeast of Watson, received 2 inches, and New London, just 10 miles north of the Willmar Treatment Center, only picked up 2 inches).  The band of snow weakened substantially as it moved east of Willmar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Few trees down.
Reported at the St. Cloud airport.
Four inch diameter branches down.
Hail fell at the golf course in the northwest portion of Hutchinson.
Tree toppled.

The second tornado from a cyclic tornadic supercell touched down on the western edge of a farmstead. It snapped a dozen trees and demolished a barn and sheds.  The roof of a house was peeled away and four vehicles were moved.

The third tornado in a series touched down in a corn field and trekked straight east. It caused minor house damage and knocked down several sheds and outbuildings, then moved across corn and bean fields before causing minor damage to a farmstead.  It dissipated shortly after moving through the farmstead. Videotaped by storm chasers.

The fourth tornado in a series formed atop a bluff on the west side of the Le Sueur River valley, struck two homes down in the river valley, then moved back up the bluffs on the east side of the valley, where it snapped many trees and produced minor damage to some more houses, sheds and outbuildings. Videotaped by storm chasers.
The fifth tornado in a series touched down in a corn field, clipped a couple of storage buildings and a lawn care center, then moved across more fields and a meadow before dissipating.
Measured by ASOS.

The sixth tornado in a series was the last of five to hit Blue Earth County.  This one took direct aim on four farmsteads.  Barns, sheds and outbuildings were downed. A roof was taken off a house and numerous trees were snapped. Law Enforcement video showed this to be a multiple vortex tornado.

Captured on video by law enforcement, this was the seventh and last in a series of tornadoes produced by a cyclic tornadic supercell.  It touched down briefly in an open field and dissipated before reaching the city of Janesville.

The third tornado in a series crossed mostly open meadows and fields, only occasionally knocking down a few trees.  This tornado was associated with the storm that produced downburst damage from 1510 to 1520 from 6 W Randall to Sobieski.
Several trees down along Blue Lake, including one on a power line.
Several trees down.

Numerous trees down, including some on vehicles and power lines. Semi truck tipped over. Bleachers at fairgrounds were ripped apart. A roof was blown off a used car building, and a few windows were blown out at an apartment complex.

The same downburst that hit east central Todd County from 1455 to 1505 (and was associated with the storm that also produced a tornado from 1500 to 1505) then briefly entered west central Morrison County.  After a short distance, it weakened, but not before several dozen trees were toppled and causing minor damage to outbuildings.
Few trees down. Light standard toppled at the High School football field.
Several trees toppled.
Several 8 to 10 inch diameter branches down.

A wide swath of damaging wind accompanied a brief tornado that touched down 7 SW Philbrook.  The NWS damage survey, however, determined that the vast majority of the damage in northeastern Todd County was due to the strong wind associated with this tornadic storm.  The swath of damaging wind was about six miles wide and 9 miles long in Todd County (it continued for another three miles in Morrison County - see corresponding entry).  Hundreds, perhaps thousands, of trees were downed by the winds that blew from northeast to southwest.  Some trees fell on houses, outbuildings, and vehicles.  Many trees were felled in the community of Philbrook.

Another wide swath of damaging wind moved through Todd County, this time affecting the east central portion.  It was associated with a three mile long tornado from 1500 to 1505 that began in Todd County and briefly entered Morrison County.  The NWS damage survey determined, however, that the vast majority of the widespread damage was due to the strong downburst.  Thousands of trees were felled, including many hundred that snapped.  Some trees fell on houses, outbuildings and vehicles.  All trees from the downburst were oriented from northeast to southwest.
Several trees down in the south side of town.

The last in a series of downbursts tore across southwestern Morrison County.  The NWS damage survey determined that although this storm produced a two mile long tornado in Morrison County from 1515 to 1520, the vast majority of the damage was due to a 12 mile long, 4 mile wide downburst.  Many hundreds of trees were downed, oriented from northeast to southwest.  The downburst also hit Flensburg and Sobieski, both of which saw minor house and outbuilding damage.

Downburst damage that began 3 S Staples (in northeastern Todd County) continued into Morrison County.  After downing hundreds of trees for three miles in the Lincoln area, it finally weakened.  The total distance traversed by the downburst was 12 miles across Todd and Morrison counties.
Hail covered ground.
Hail covered ground. Leaves stripped.
Few trees and power lines down.
Several 8 to 10 inch diameter branches down.
Sheet of plywood was lifted up and blown into a transformer.
Few trees and power lines down.
Scattered trees and power lines down.
Several 12 inch diameter branches felled.
Few trees down, including one which broke a window.
Tornado touched down on Forest Lake, then briefly moved ashore before dissipating. A boat lift was flipped and several large branches were downed.
Tornado tore across corn fields and several groves of trees. Videotaped by storm chasers.

The same storm that produced a tornado 7 SW Philbrook as well as downburst damage from 3 S Staples to 2 SE Lincoln also produced large hail that fell in the Randall area.  Many fields of corn and beans were decimated.
Few trees down.
Numerous trees down, including one on a house. Boats and docks were thrown out of the water on the east side of Lake Minnewaska.
About one dozen trees downed.
Numerous trees down, roof off a barn; machine shed blown about 100 feet.
Tree downed, brushing house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved out of the southern high plains toward eastern Wisconsin. Heavy snow and strong winds tracked from southwest and west central Minnesota into west central Wisconsin. There was a wide swath of 6 to 10 inches of snow, most of which fell prior to 1600 CST. During the afternoon of the 23rd and into the early morning hours of the 24th, winds gusted around 35 mph creating near-blizzard conditions, chiefly in open country of western and southern Minnesota.  Many counties recommended no travel, and numerous schools were closed. Several hundred accidents occurred, especially around the Minneapolis-St. Paul metropolitan area.  The top snowfall total, 10 inches, fell at Canby, Long Prairie, Little Falls, and Onamia.  Eight inches were registered at Dawson, Benson, Willmar, Buffalo and Milaca. Six inches were reported as far south as St. James, Mankato, and Savage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Few trees toppled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wide swath of 6 to 10 inches, with a few pockets of 11 and 12 inches, swept from southwest Minnesota, across the Minneapolis-St. Paul area, into west central Wisconsin.  Winds across this area were 25 to 30 mph, with blowing and drifting snow in open areas.  Although some parts of far south central Minnesota only picked up 4 to 6 inches, winds in this area were a little stronger, creating near-blizzard conditions. The greatest snowfall totals were in the Twin Cities metro, where Chaska, Chanhassen and New Hope all picked up 11 inches. Ten inches were recorded at Lamberton, Springfield and Gaylord. There was a sharp cutoff on the northern edge of the snow; e.g. Lamberton in southernmost Redwood County, tallied 10 inches, while Belview, 25 miles to the north in far northern Redwood County, only saw 2 inches. Rockford, straddling the Hennepin/Wright County line, received 6 inches, whereas Buffalo, 10 miles to the northwest in central Wright County, noted only 1 inch.
Heavy snow of 6 to 10 inches fell across Big Stone and Traverse counties. Dumont received 6 inches of snow with 10 inches at Ortonville.
Heavy snow of 6 to 10 inches fell across Big Stone and Traverse counties. Dumont received 6 inches of snow with 10 inches at Ortonville.
Heavy snow of 6 to 9 inches fell across Big Stone County. Nine inches fell in Ortonville.
Six to seven inches of snow fell over overnight. The snow in southern Lake County was enhanced by Lake Superior and higher terrain. Finland had 6 inches of snow.
Six to seven inches of snow fell over overnight. The snow in southern Lake County was enhanced by Lake Superior and higher terrain. Finland had 6 inches of snow.

Strong winds were the main feature with this winter storm. Snowfall ranged from two to four inches, but winds frequently gusted over 35 mph, producing numerous whiteout conditions in open country. Snow plows were pulled off the roads in many counties during the evening and overnight hours.  The snow and winds were due to low pressure moving from Colorado into Iowa, then into Wisconsin.

Freezing drizzle and freezing rain produced icy roads. The freezing precipitation was followed by a snowfall of 3 to 6 inches. The snow was accompanied by wind gusts over 40 mph. The wind caused visibilities to drop to less than a quarter mile at times in blowing snow...and also caused drifting snow. The combination of freezing precipitation, snow, and strong winds also caused downed electrical lines and resulting power outages...especially in Murray and Nobles Counties.

Snowfall varying from 3 to 8 inches occurred over most of southwest Minnesota, with the 8 inch report from Ivanhoe. The snowfall was accompanied by freezing rain and freezing drizzle. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph caused blowing and drifting snow, which reduced visibility to a quarter mile in places and also blocked roads. Other roads were very icy, which resulted in several accidents and vehicles sliding into ditches.

There was a sharp cutoff on the northern edge of the snow; e.g. Lamberton in southernmost Redwood County, tallied 10 inches, while Belview, 25 miles to the north in far northern Redwood County, only saw 2 inches. Rockford, straddling the Hennepin/Wright County line, received 6 inches, whereas Buffalo, 10 miles to the northwest in central Wright County, noted only 1 inch.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on the NWS damage survey, the vast majority of damage in Lake Crystal this day was due to strong thunderstorm winds, rather than the brief tornado that also touched down (see corresponding entry).  Dozens of trees were toppled, some snapped. Many trees fell on sheds, homes and vehicles.
Measured by MNDOT Road Weather System.

A multiple vortex tornado touched down west of Pennock near a turkey farm, which was severely damaged. 200 to 300 turkeys were killed.  The tornado then tracked straight east through the city of Pennock, knocking down trees and causing some damage to houses, including partially removing the roof from one home.  A large storage building was destroyed in Pennock, as were several sheds and a garage.  Just east of Pennock, it took direct aim on a house, lifting off its roof and blowing a shed into a nearby field.  Another garage nearby had its roof removed. A strong rear flank downdraft accompanied the tornado, producing wind damage from just east of Pennock to Willmar (see corresponding entry).

A strong rear flank downdraft accompanied the Pennock tornado (see corresponding entry).  Several trees were felled, bleachers at an athletic complex were destroyed, a grain bin collapsed onto a Bobcat, and a sign at the Kandi Mall was knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 2 to 4 inches was accompanied by freezing rain at the beginning of the event. Roads were very slippery, causing numerous vehicles to slide off the road and several accidents.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gust measured by MNDOT Road Weather System 9 S Starbuck.  Hundreds of trees were snapped or downed at Glacial Lakes State Park, which was nearly full of campers for the holiday weekend. Falling trees caused minor injuries to two campers.  Nearby, five barns were downed and several empty grain bins were blown over.  The RWIS was still measuring a gust of 61 knots at 0013 CST.
Scattered trees down.

Hundreds of trees down in the city of Morris; some fell on houses and vehicles. Three barns were damaged at the West Central Research Center on the north side of town.  Gust measured by MNDOT AWOS.
Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved out of the southern high plains toward eastern Wisconsin. Heavy snow and strong winds tracked from southwest and west central Minnesota into west central Wisconsin. There was a wide swath of 6 to 10 inches of snow, most of which fell prior to 1600 CST. During the afternoon of the 23rd and into the early morning hours of the 24th, winds gusted around 35 mph creating near-blizzard conditions, chiefly in open country of western and southern Minnesota.  Many counties recommended no travel, and numerous schools were closed. Several hundred accidents occurred, especially around the Minneapolis-St. Paul metropolitan area.  The top snowfall total, 10 inches, fell at Canby, Long Prairie, Little Falls, and Onamia.  Eight inches were registered at Dawson, Benson, Willmar, Buffalo and Milaca. Six inches were reported as far south as St. James, Mankato, and Savage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought 6 to 8 inches of snow to parts of southeast Minnesota.  Strong northwest winds at 20 to 30 mph caused considerable blowing and drifting, which made travel hazardous.  Specific snowfall totals from weather observers included 7.7 inches at Mantorville (Dodge County), 7.3 inches at Lake City (Wabasha County) and 6.3 inches at Oronoco (Olmsted County).

A surface low tracked from Missouri into northern Wisconsin, where it deepened over the Great Lakes.  A very strong temperature and moisture gradient set up over eastern South Dakota into central Minnesota, where the heaviest snow fell.  4 to 10 inches of snow brushed portions of Grant, eastern Otter Tail, Wadena, and Hubbard counties.  The most snow, 10 inches, was reported 4 miles west of Staples.  North winds increased to 20 to 30 mph, with gusts to 35 mph, as the low moved into the Great Lakes.

Strong low pressure moved out of the southern high plains toward eastern Wisconsin. Heavy snow and strong winds tracked from southwest and west central Minnesota into west central Wisconsin. There was a wide swath of 6 to 10 inches of snow, most of which fell prior to 1600 CST. During the afternoon of the 23rd and into the early morning hours of the 24th, winds gusted around 35 mph creating near-blizzard conditions, chiefly in open country of western and southern Minnesota.  Many counties recommended no travel, and numerous schools were closed. Several hundred accidents occurred, especially around the Minneapolis-St. Paul metropolitan area.  The top snowfall total, 10 inches, fell at Canby, Long Prairie, Little Falls, and Onamia.  Eight inches were registered at Dawson, Benson, Willmar, Buffalo and Milaca. Six inches were reported as far south as St. James, Mankato, and Savage.

An Alberta Clipper low dropped into western North Dakota, then reformed over southeast Minnesota.  As it reformed and strengthened, it produced a band of 4 to 7 inches of snow over portions of the northwest quarter of Minnesota.  Northwest winds increased behind the low, gusting from 25 to 35 mph.  As temperatures hovered around freezing during the event, blowing snow never became a big problem.

Six to almost seven inches of lake and terrain-enhanced snow fell over the head of Lake Superior. Another band of heavy snow of 6 to 8 inches fell from about Bigfork through the Iron Range to Tower. Ten miles northwest of Two Harbors had 8.5", Bigfork had 8", while Cloquet, Adolph, and 15 miles north of Duluth all had 7" of snow.

A winter storm brought 6 to 8 inches of snow to parts of southeast Minnesota.  Strong northwest winds at 20 to 30 mph caused considerable blowing and drifting, which made travel hazardous.  Specific snowfall totals from weather observers included 7.7 inches at Mantorville (Dodge County), 7.3 inches at Lake City (Wabasha County) and 6.3 inches at Oronoco (Olmsted County).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Lexington and 109th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic front moved through the northern plains, and produced strong northwest winds.  The strongest winds occurred across the northern Red River Valley, where a MN DOT RWIS site at St. Vincent, MN, gusted up to 56 mph.  Little snow was associated with the frontal passage, but there was about an inch of fluffy snow on the ground at the time.  The worst visibilities were found in open country.  With little falling snow and mostly sunny conditions above the blowing snow layer, it was considered a ground blizzard.  A slow improvement occurred in the afternoon, with the northern Red River Valley actually out of blizzard conditions by 3 pm.  Quite a bit of dirt mixed in with the blowing snow.

The first significant snowfall of the season arrived very late in the season, waiting until Groundhog Day.  An 80 mile wide swath of heavy snow fell from west central Minnesota into west central and northwest Wisconsin (see corresponding entry). Six to eleven inches were common due to low pressure moving from Kansas to northern Illinois.  The top snowfall total that was reported was 11 inches at Montevideo and New London. Ten inches were measured at Eden Valley, Forest Lake, Ham Lake, Hutchinson and Litchfield. Some other notable totals include 9 inches at Benson, Buffalo, Fridley and Princeton, and 8 inches at Albany, Belview, Olivia and Watertown.  Winds were typically 20 to 25 mph, and though there was some blowing snow, it was not a major problem.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused widespread tree damage. Several houses were damaged by falling trees and wind blown tree debris. A roof was blown off a home northwest of Brewster.

A strong and fast moving cold front plowed out of the Canadian Prairies and into Minnesota, bringing a quick snow that totaled two to three inches.  The powdery snow was whipped around by winds frequently gusting over 45 mph producing near-zero visibility.  Whiteout conditions were prevalent throughout the open terrain of west central and south central Minnesota.  Law Enforcement recommended no travel and virtually all schools closed early.  The highest wind gust, measured by AWOS, was 60 mph at Fairmont airport.  Some other notable wind gust maxima included 51 mph at Faribault, 47 mph at Waseca, Owatonna and St. James, and 46 mph in Redwood Falls.

A fairly compact ridge of high pressure brought clear skies and brisk west/northwest winds to portions of northwest and west central Minnesota.  High temperatures on the 8th were only in the single digits above and below zero.  The west/northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph with a few gusts to 35 mph kept temperatures from plunging too far, yet overnight temperatures did drop to 10 below to 20 below zero (or about 30 degrees below normal).  This dropped wind chill temperatures to 40 below to 50 below zero for a prolonged period the night of the 8th into the morning of the 9th.  With only a few inches of snow left on the ground (mostly in sheltered areas), these extreme wind chill temperatures disrupted public events and services.  Some schools cancelled or postponed activities, rather than risk unneeded exposure to these elements.  Numerous water main breaks were also reported, due to the prolonged exposure to the cold.

High temperatures on the 8th were only in the single digits above and below zero.  The west/northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph with a few gusts to 35 mph kept temperatures from plunging too far, yet overnight temperatures did drop to 10 below to 20 below zero (or about 30 degrees below normal).  This dropped wind chill temperatures to 40 below to 50 below zero for a prolonged period the night of the 8th into the morning of the 9th.  With only a few inches of snow left on the ground (mostly in sheltered areas), these extreme wind chill temperatures disrupted public events and services.  Some schools cancelled or postponed activities, rather than risk unneeded exposure to these elements.  Numerous water main breaks were also reported, due to the prolonged exposure to the cold.

Sustained winds over 40 mph with gusts around 65 mph in places developed at the beginning of the afternoon and persisted for 3 to 4 hours before gradually abating. The high winds blew over the roof tresses and wall framework of an industrial building under construction. One man received shoulder injuries and was transported to a hospital when he was pinned under the falling debris. The high winds also caused low visibilities in blowing snow as they accompanied a light snowfall. The low visibilities caused early closings of schools.

A strong upper level disturbance combined with an inverted trough at the surface to produce heavy snow across northwest Minnesota.  The event began as light rain, then changed over to snow as cold air was drawn into the system.  The heavy, wet snow broke some tree branches and resulted in scattered power outages.  A foot of snow fell from the Lake of the Woods to Thief Lake, with 6 to 10 inches of snow common across the rest of the warning area.  The heavy snow moved very slowly from west to east, resulting in a slow eastward progression of warnings.  Therefore, the heavy snow warning was issued first for Kittson and western Marshall counties (3/26/03 at 10:50 pm cst), next for Roseau and eastern Marshall counties (3/27/03 at 3:58 am cst), and last for Lake of the Woods and northern Beltrami counties (3/27/03 at 7:34 am cst).  The warnings for Kittson, east and west Marshall, and Roseau were cancelled at 8:33 am cst on 3/27/03, while Lake of the Woods and northern Beltrami were cancelled at 11:30 am cst on 3/27/03.

Snowfall of 3 to 6 inches was accompanied by areas of low visibility in blowing snow from strong winds. Icing occurred when moisture from the initially wet snow froze as temperatures fell. Travel was slowed and some schools were delayed in opening on Monday February third.

The first significant snowfall of the season arrived very late in the season, waiting until Groundhog Day.  An 80 mile wide swath of heavy snow fell from west central Minnesota into west central and northwest Wisconsin (see corresponding entry). Six to eleven inches were common due to low pressure moving from Kansas to northern Illinois.  The top snowfall total that was reported was 11 inches at Montevideo and New London. Ten inches were measured at Eden Valley, Forest Lake, Ham Lake, Hutchinson and Litchfield. Some other notable totals include 9 inches at Benson, Buffalo, Fridley and Princeton, and 8 inches at Albany, Belview, Olivia and Watertown.  Winds were typically 20 to 25 mph, and though there was some blowing snow, it was not a major problem.

A strong and fast moving cold front plowed out of the Canadian Prairies and into Minnesota, bringing a quick snow that totaled two to three inches.  The powdery snow was whipped around by winds frequently gusting over 45 mph producing near-zero visibility.  Whiteout conditions were prevalent throughout the open terrain of west central and south central Minnesota.  Law Enforcement recommended no travel and virtually all schools closed early.  The highest wind gust, measured by AWOS, was 60 mph at Fairmont airport.  Some other notable wind gust maxima included 51 mph at Faribault, 47 mph at Waseca, Owatonna and St. James, and 46 mph in Redwood Falls.

A narrow band of heavy snow fell from northeastern Koochiching County into far northwestern St. Louis County. Snow amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches. International Falls had 9 inches while Ranier had 12 inches of snow.

A combination of synoptic and lake-enhanced snow fell across the area with amounts ranging from seven inches in Cloquet to 12 inches in West Duluth. Other amounts include 10 inches at Finland, 8 inches near Grand Marais, 7 inches up the Gunflint Trail, and 10.5 inches at Floodwood.

Low pressure tracked from west to east across Missouri, with the northern fringe of heavy snow impacting southern Minnesota.  10 to 14 inches were common along Interstate 90.  Winds gusted around 30 mph causing some blowing and drifting snow. Most schools were canceled and some businesses closed. Totals included 14 inches in Fairmont and Albert Lea, 12 inches in Blue Earth, 11 inches Winnebago and 10 inches in St. James.  The heavy snow did not extend very far north into Minnesota, as Mankato picked up only 3 inches, with 2 inches in Waseca and Owatonna, and only a trace in Springfield.

Low pressure tracked from west to east across Missouri, with the northern fringe of heavy snow impacting southern Minnesota.  10 to 14 inches were common along Interstate 90.  Winds gusted around 30 mph causing some blowing and drifting snow. Most schools were canceled and some businesses closed. Totals included 14 inches in Fairmont and Albert Lea, 12 inches in Blue Earth, 11 inches Winnebago and 10 inches in St. James.  The heavy snow did not extend very far north into Minnesota, as Mankato picked up only 3 inches, with 2 inches in Waseca and Owatonna, and only a trace in Springfield.

A fairly compact ridge of high pressure brought clear skies and brisk west/northwest winds to portions of northwest and west central Minnesota.  High temperatures on the 8th were only in the single digits above and below zero.  The west/northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph with a few gusts to 35 mph kept temperatures from plunging too far, yet overnight temperatures did drop to 10 below to 20 below zero (or about 30 degrees below normal).  This dropped wind chill temperatures to 40 below to 50 below zero for a prolonged period the night of the 8th into the morning of the 9th.  With only a few inches of snow left on the ground (mostly in sheltered areas), these extreme wind chill temperatures disrupted public events and services.  Some schools cancelled or postponed activities, rather than risk unneeded exposure to these elements.  Numerous water main breaks were also reported, due to the prolonged exposure to the cold.

The first significant snowfall of the season arrived very late in the season, waiting until Groundhog Day.  An 80 mile wide swath of heavy snow fell from west central Minnesota into west central and northwest Wisconsin (see corresponding entry). Six to eleven inches were common due to low pressure moving from Kansas to northern Illinois.  The top snowfall total that was reported was 11 inches at Montevideo and New London. Ten inches were measured at Eden Valley, Forest Lake, Ham Lake, Hutchinson and Litchfield. Some other notable totals include 9 inches at Benson, Buffalo, Fridley and Princeton, and 8 inches at Albany, Belview, Olivia and Watertown.  Winds were typically 20 to 25 mph, and though there was some blowing snow, it was not a major problem.
Freezing rain changed quickly to snow which produced accumulations of 2 to 5 inches. The slippery conditions slowed travel and caused a few minor accidents.

A strong upper level disturbance combined with an inverted trough at the surface to produce heavy snow across northwest Minnesota.  The event began as light rain, then changed over to snow as cold air was drawn into the system.  The heavy, wet snow broke some tree branches and resulted in scattered power outages.  A foot of snow fell from the Lake of the Woods to Thief Lake, with 6 to 10 inches of snow common across the rest of the warning area.  The heavy snow moved very slowly from west to east, resulting in a slow eastward progression of warnings.  Therefore, the heavy snow warning was issued first for Kittson and western Marshall counties (3/26/03 at 10:50 pm cst), next for Roseau and eastern Marshall counties (3/27/03 at 3:58 am cst), and last for Lake of the Woods and northern Beltrami counties (3/27/03 at 7:34 am cst).  The warnings for Kittson, east and west Marshall, and Roseau were cancelled at 8:33 am cst on 3/27/03, while Lake of the Woods and northern Beltrami were cancelled at 11:30 am cst on 3/27/03.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado touched down just west of an oats field, moved across a grassy field, crossed the Cottonwood River, then dissipated.  There were a few broken trees near the Cottonwood River, but no damage was caused to any structure.  The oats were just coming out of the ground and were laid down only for about 12 hours before returning to normal growth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early spring snowstorm impacted southeast Minnesota, producing accumulations of 6 to 10 inches.  The heaviest snowfall was in locations near the Minnesota-Iowa border, which included 10.5 and 10 inches respectively at Brownsdale and Austin (Mower County).  Canton and Whalan (Fillmore County) both received 10 inches, while 9.2 inches was reported at Stewartville (Olmsted County).  East winds gusting to 30 mph caused near whiteout conditions at times.

Warmer air advected in over the colder air at the surface, and helped to wring out moisture in the form of snow across northwest Minnesota.  An upper level disturbance also helped to strengthen the lift.  Snow began falling by late afternoon and early evening on the 3rd, with the heavy snow warning criteria reached the morning of the 4th.  6 to 8 inches of snow fell across the 5 county area.

A mixture of sleet and freezing rain fell, causing an icy glaze up to 1/2" to accumulate on roads, trees, and power lines. In addition to the precipitation, the head of the lakes area had very strong winds sustained at 35 to 50 mph.  The Aerial Lift Bridge in Duluth reported gusts up to 66 mph. The peak wind at the Duluth Airport was 56 mph.  The strong winds closed the port entry of Duluth as the strong east winds packed ice into the ship canal.  Numerous trees and and power lines were blown down. The KMart at the Miller Hill Mall location lost part of its roof in the high winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first of three confirmed funnel clouds and one tornado on May 14 developed near Sleepy Eye.  The airmass was very cold aloft, a weak surface trough was draped over south central Minnesota, and partly cloudy skies during the late morning hours enabled the development of scattered thunderstorms.
This tornado originated in Kandiyohi County, moving into Meeker County at 1916 CST.  It tracked across open fields, damaging only a few trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms near the Minnesota Iowa border produced hail the size of dimes and quarters.  Spotters estimated wind gusts of 60 mph near Lyle (Mower County), while a funnel cloud was reported near Austin (Mower County) by KAAL-TV.

Thunderstorms near the Minnesota Iowa border produced hail the size of dimes and quarters.  Spotters estimated wind gusts of 60 mph near Lyle (Mower County), while a funnel cloud was reported near Austin (Mower County) by KAAL-TV.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: County wide flooding.  Five to ten inches of rain were reported across much of Meeker, with the heaviest rainfall amounts across the southern tier.  Ten inches were registered between Cedar Mills and Cosmos, along with 5 to 7 inches common from Lake Minnie Belle to Grover City.  A number of roads were flooded, and many crops were under water.
Several trees down.
The fourth tornado in a series developed 20 minutes after Buffalo Lake was hit. It touched down briefly in Sibley County, tracked across an open field before moving into Renville County at 1912 CST.
Measured by Skywarn spotter.

County wide flooding.  Four to eight inches were common across the region, with a number of county and township roads under water.  Basements were flooded in the city of Kandiyohi, along with 12 blocks under water in Atwater.  A number of cars were stalled due to flooding in a corridor from Wilmar to Atwater.  Many crops were also under water.

County wide flooding.  After two bouts of heavy rain on June 22 and 23, three to four additional inches fell, resulting in widespread street and basement flooding in Glenwood.  The sewer system was also overloaded.  Four familes in the northwest section of Glenwood were evacuated when the water level reached a depth of four feet in their neighborhood.

County wide flooding.  Several periods of heavy rain produced 8 to 12 inched across Morrison county.  Highway 371 was flooded between Little Falls and Randall, along with Highway 27 being closed from little Falls to Pierz when a culvert washed away and caused partial collapse of the road.  Many other county and township roads were also closed due to flooding, along with numerous basements and driveways being washed out.  Just North of Little Falls, two businesses were flooded and one was surrounded by water.
This tornado moved in from Renville County at 1755, then dissipated as it moved alongside a farmstead. A few trees were knocked down, and there was minor property damage.
The second tornado in a series touched down briefly in Sibley County before crossing into Renville County at 1831 CST.

This second tornado entered Renville County at 1831 CST, then remained nearly stationary in an open field for about 10 minutes. Then it began moving northeast again, crossing Highway 212, then looping back to the south as the tornado roped out.  The only damage was to some power lines and transformers.

This fourth tornado entered Renville County at 1912 CST and hit portions of one farmstead. Sheds, outbuildings and grain bins were damaged, a barn was flattened, and a number of trees were downed.  The tornado dissipated before it reached Highway 212.

The fifth and final tornado in two hours to hit the Buffalo Lake area touched down 7 miles north of Buffalo Lake, or 1.5 miles northeast of Churchill.  It traveled straight north, occasionally downing trees.
A brief tornado caused no reported damage.
Roof off part of garage in northeast section of Fairmont. Several trees down, including one on a house.
40 acres of beans shredded.
Semi blown over on Interstate 90.
Crop losses estimated by Extension Office and ASCS Office.
Numerous trees down around Alexandria, especially around Lake Carlos and Lake Le Homme Dieu.  Some fell on cars and houses. Measured sustained wind reached 47 knots at 1239 CST.

The first of five tornadoes to hit the Buffalo Lake area this evening, the tornado hit three farmsteads before crossing into Sibley County at 1755 CST.  Trees were knocked down, sheds and outbuildings destroyed, a grain dryer was overturned, a grain bin and silo were destroyed, and houses sustained minor damage.
This was the first of five tornadoes from one supercell in Kandiyohi County, all of which were videotaped by a storm chaser. This first tornado touched down briefly in an open field.
The second of five tornadoes touched down in an open field.
The third tornado in a series also touched down in an open field. A few trees downed.
The fourth tornado was another brief touchdown in an open field.

The fifth and final tornado from the same supercell moved straight north for two miles, then turned and moved from east to west for one mile as it roped out.  During its northward trek, it damaged three farmsteads, knocking down sheds, outbuildings and trees.  While traveling west, it moved across corn and bean fields.
Numerous trees down around Miltona and Lake Irene, including one on a garage and another on a deck. One tree was blown down onto a van, but the family inside the van was not injured.
Hail fell on newly planted crops.
Scattered trees down, including one on a vehicle.
Numerous fields in two townships were hit with golf ball size hail along with winds around 45 knots. Crop losses estimated by Douglas County agriculture officials.
Ten inch diameter tree felled.
Numerous large branches down along with a few trees and power lines.
Twelve inch diameter tree toppled.
Large branches down. Few trees down blocking road.
Six inch tree top down at Valley View and Dell.
Several trees down in northeast Minneapolis.
Tree blown down onto house.
Boatlift lifted out of the water and blown over a dock then about 200 feet down the shore to another house.
County wide flooding.  Several inches of rainfall.  Highway 27 was flooded northwest of Kensington.  Street flooding and roads under water in Alexandria and Nelson.
Several secondary and township roads washed out.  Highway 71 between Browerville and Long Prairie was under water.

Numerous trees down in a wide swath that originated in Kandiyohi County and extended into Meeker, Renville and McLeod Counties.  Sheds and outbuildings were blown over across an approximate area of 40 to 50 square miles.  Several grain bins were damaged, and the roof of the Cedar Mills liquor store was removed. The damage survey revealed that winds were from the north-northwest.
Tornado moved from Yellow Medicine County into Chippewa County at 1939 CST.  A roof was blown off a shed, several trees were downed, and two gravity boxes were pushed into the side of a building.
About one dozen trees were felled. Minor damage to a house.

This was the sixth tornado on this day to touch down in Kandiyohi County, although the other five tornadoes were from a different supercell.  This tornado tore across open fields then moved into Meeker County at 1916 CST.  A few trees were felled.

A wide swath of wind originated in Kandiyohi County and swept across Meeker, Renville and McLeod Counties. Numerous trees were downed, sheds and outbuildings blown over, several barns were flattened, and grain bins blown over. About 50 farmsteads were damaged across an approximate area of 30 to 40 square miles.
Flooding occurred in northern portion of county.  New London recieved three inches of rain in 30 minutes.  Parking lots and interesctions were flooded in Spicer.
Videotaped by storm chasers.
Tree down on Gateway Trail near Westminster.
Minor damage to corn.
Hail storms pummeled bean, corn and wheat fields of about 60 farmers in Stevens County June 21-24. Damage amount compiled by Stevens County Emergency Management.

County wide flooding observed with  5 to 10 inches reported in western and northern sections of Hennepin. The maximum amount of rain (10 inches),  was recorded in Rogers, and in Hassen Township.  A house along the South side of the Crow River was surrounded by water. A storage barn on the property, which contained classic automobiles, was swamped with two feet of water.  Many basements and streets were also flooded in Maple Grove, Rogers, Plymoth, and Champlin.  The intersection of Highway 100 and France Avenue in Brooklyn Center was also closed.
Five inch diameter branches downed.

Wind equipment at MSP International Airport measured 73 mph. At another part of the airfield, NWS ASOS recorded a peak gust of 51 knots. Seventeen commercial aircraft were either moved or partially damaged.

County wide flooding.  Several periods of heavy rain produced 8 to 12 inches across much of Douglas county.  Numerous roads were under water in the Alexandria, Nelson, and Carlos areas.  Some cars actually floated in Alexandria and a few houses were flooded.  Three feet of water was in the Alexandria Hospital's boiler room after the sewer system overloaded.  Some roads were still flooded near the Long Prairie River until the morning of June 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported by the public near Rose Creek.
There were several reports of funnel clouds over Lake City and Lake Pepin.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief touchdown on the southeast shore of Lake Minnewaska. Tornado flipped over a camper and knocked down trees, some of which fell on the roof of a cabin.
Hundreds of trees downed from Edina to Richfield, including across south Minneapolis and Bloomington.  Many trees fell on cars and houses while many other trees blocked roads.
Few trees down.
Several eight inch branches down in northern part of Buffalo.
A few trees downed.
Several trees down. Flagpole blown off a building.
Numerous trees down, including some on a camper, a house and a garage.
Golf ball size hail landed on the trucks being used by the local fire department during weather spotting.

Scattered trees down. This was the beginning of a large swath of wind damage that extended into Meeker, Renville and McLeod Counties between 2025 and 2110 CST. Winds in this wide swath were from the west-northwest.
Lightning struck a large tree, destroying the tree. The lightning also damaged appliances in at least two homes, and caused a power outage over a small area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms near the Minnesota Iowa border produced hail the size of dimes and quarters.  Spotters estimated wind gusts of 60 mph near Lyle (Mower County), while a funnel cloud was reported near Austin (Mower County) by KAAL-TV.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down.
A children's playhouse was lifted and blown some distance, where it landed on a truck being driven along Highway 212. One man was slightly injured when some of the smashed glass landed in his eye.

The Southern portion of Sherburne county flooded.  Five to ten inches of precipitation were common, including 8.19 inches measured in Elk River.  Some township roads were washed out, along with street flooding in and near Elk River.  Low Lying areas near the Elk River were also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms near the Minnesota Iowa border produced hail the size of dimes and quarters.  Spotters estimated wind gusts of 60 mph near Lyle (Mower County), while a funnel cloud was reported near Austin (Mower County) by KAAL-TV.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees down.
Scattered trees felled.
Several trees down.
County wide flooding.  Three to five inches of rain caused many roads and basements to flood in the Forest Lake and Hugo areas.

County wide flooding.  Three  to six inches of rain fell within a few hours, which resulted in numerous reports of street and basement flooding.  Several feet of water covered Larpenteur Avenue between Hamline and I-94.

The third tornado in a series touched down in Sibley County and moved straight north, occasionally stalling in place for minutes at a time.  It heavily damaged two farmsteads, where all outbuildings and sheds were destroyed, and the houses sustained significant damage.   It entered Renville County at 1845 CST and then into Buffalo Lake.
Two foot diameter trees blown down. Grain bin blown down.

This third tornado entered Renville County at 1845 CST and entered the city of Buffalo Lake, where damage was significant.  Five people sustained minor injuries.  Roofs were blown off the Lutheran Church, as well as a number of homes and businesses. Sheds and garages were blown down, a grain elevator was destroyed, and hundreds of trees were broken.  Video and a storm survey determined the tornado was multiple vortex. 29 houses were damaged beyond repair, 51 sustained major damage, and 120 more suffered minor damage.
Numerous large oak trees toppled.
Brief touchdown on north side of Glacial Lakes State Park. Few trees damaged.
Brief touchdown on the southwest side of the Minnesota River, with minor tree damage. The tornado then crossed into Chippewa County at 1939 CST.
Several six inch diameter branches down.
Few trees down, including one on a car and truck.
Scattered trees toppled.
Several eight inch branches down.

This was the last of the damage from a wide swath of wind that swept across Kandiyohi, Meeker and Renville Counties, beginning at 2025 CST in those counties.  Scattered trees and power lines were downed, as were a few sheds and outbuildings.

County wide flooding.  Several periods of rain produced 8 to 12 inches across Todd county.  This excessive rainfall caused basements to flood, along with numerous secondary and township roads  flooded and some washed out.  Highway 71 between Browerville and Long Prairie was also under water.  The Long Prairie River rose to one of its highest levels since 1972, flooding several homes and businesses in Long Prairie.  Some homes and roads along the north end of Lake Osakis were also flooded.

County wide flooding.  Four to eight inches of rainfall were reported across the county.  Some township roads were covered by water, with closures widespread along Highway 95 between North Branch and Taylors Falls.  Highway 15 was also closed near Almelund.
High winds, resulting from the combination of a strong pressure gradient and previous thunderstorms, blew down trees, and caused minor home and vehicle damage.
A 20 inch diameter tree was blown across Highway 92.
The MN Highway Patrol reported water over Highway 10.
Street flooding was reported in south Moorhead.
4 inch diameter branches were blown down.

This tornado originated west of Pembina, North Dakota.  It tracked southeast and crossed the Red River about 2 miles south of Pembina, ND.  It then continued on a one-half mile track in Kittson county.  Several tree tops were broken off along the path.
Two large trees were blown over.
Tree branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms brought 5 to 8 inches of rain to portions of Wilkin county.  The underpass along highway 75 near Kent had water over the road for several days.  The county also reported water over other rural roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the NOAA weather radio tower and knocked the broadcast off the air.
5 inch diameter branches were broken off.
Street flooding was reported.

A 7 year-old girl was injured after lightning struck her family's home.  The lightning hit a television antenna, cracked the ceiling, and burned a wall behind the girl.  The strike also started a fire in the dining room.  The girl suffered minor burns on her back and right shoulder.
Street flooding was reported.
The west door of a shed was blown in and pieces of roof were scattered for a third of a mile into a field.
Street flooding was reported.
Several trees were uprooted and numerous branches were blown down.
Several large tree branches were blown down.
Water flowed across Highway 59.
Scattered large limbs down brought down power lines.
Scattered trees down, including some on houses and power lines.
Roof partially blown off home. Barn blown down. Numerous trees and large branches down.
A MN DOT RWIS site along Highway 75 measured the wind gust.
Numerous trees down, including some on houses, sheds, vehicles and power lines.  Combination of strong wind and large hail destroyed an estimated 9000 acres of corn and beans.
Large trees were blown down.
The hail occurred in the town of Quiring.
10 inch diameter tree branches were blown down.
Large hail severely damaged crops in the area, particularly corn, with bruising of stalks being the main damage. The hail also dented some vehicles. The amount of damage was not known.
Large hail caused severe damage to corn and soybean crops in the area. The amount of crop damage was not known.

Large hail severely damaged crops, especially corn. Many fields were deemed a total loss. The wind blown hail also damaged vehicles, cracked windows, and damaged the siding of homes. At least one greenhouse was heavily damaged. The hail was blown into drifts several inches high in many places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Street flooding up to 2 feet deep was reported along 5th Avenue NW, 20th Street NW, and Central Avenue.  A car was totalled near Cabelas due to a stopped drain when the water level rose 2.5 feet high.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were blown down.

Thunderstorm winds caused widespread tree damage, including numerous trees blown down. Falling trees damaged roofs of houses and destroyed the topper of a pickup truck, and severely damaged another pickup. Power lines were blown down, resulting in power outages. A large storage shed was destroyed. The roof of a house was blown off, and other roof damage to structures was reported.
A brief touchdown was reported.
Slow moving thunderstorms dropped 2 to 4 inches of rain in 10 minutes.  The water rose as high as car tires on 30th Avenue South.
Trees were blown down.
Numerous large trees were blown down.  A row of 10 wooden power poles were also snapped off.  Roofing and gutters were torn off two homes.
Large branches were blown down north of Erskine.  A nearby MN DOT RWIS site measured a 50 knot wind gust.
Large branches were blown down and power outages were reported.
Tree limbs were blown down.
A 6 inch diameter tree was blown down.
Large tree branches were blown down.  A 17 foot boat and a trailer were blown about a half a block.
Large branches were blown down.  A nearby MN DOT RWIS site measured a 50 knot wind gust.
Eight inch diameter trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cabin received moderate damage to its deck, siding, and insulation after being struck by lightning.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs were blown down.
A tornado briefly touched down in an open field.
The hail occurred over downtown Moorhead.

Flooding occurred in the northeastern portion of Renville county.  Four to eight inches of rain were common, resulting in some township roads  under water.  Seven and one half inches of rain was reported5 miles north of Buffalo Lake.  Many crops were under water.
6 inch diameter branches were blown down.
Trees were blown down and power outages were reported.
Water flowed over Highway 75 near the overpass.
Water flowed over several rural roads.
Water filled the underpass on Highway 75 near Kent.

County wide flooding.  Six to twelve inches of rain were unofficaially recorded across much of Wright county.  The heaviest rainfall was in the eastern portions of the county.  Many streets were impassable in the areas around Albertville, Otsego, and St Michael.  Some roads were washed out.  Many basements were flooded, including a few where the water level reached the basement ceiling.  Sandbagging of homes was necessary at the Albert Villas in Albertville.  Many crops were under water.

County wide flooding.  Five to ten inches of rain were reported, including one unofficial report of 10 inches about 6 miles northwest of Hutchinson.  A number of township and secondary roads were flooded, crops were under water, and a bridge was closed near Biscay.
Wind driven hail destroyed several sections of crops in southwest Norman county.
An NDAWN mesonet wind sensor measured a 85 mph wind gust.
This tornado had a 1 mile intermittent track along which several large trees were blown down.  One tree was sheared off nearly to the ground.  Tin was also turned up along a shed roof.
A waterspout formed over Lower Red Lake for about 30 seconds.
A large tree was blown down.
6 inch diameter tree branches were blown off.
6 inch diameter tree limbs were broken off by the strong wind.
18 inch diameter trees were uprooted and a house window was shattered by the wind driven hail.
12 inch diameter trees were blown down, some pulling out the roots as well.
A large swath of 18 inch diameter trees were blown down.
Many vehicle and house windows were broken by the large hail.  Power lines were downed in the Greenbush, Badger, and Wannaska areas.
A mobile home was shifted from its foundation and its skirt was ripped off.

A wildfire burned 300 acres of grassland and also burned some small sheds. The fire came close to five homesteads, burning to less than 20 yards from two of them. One home had smoke damage from the fire. Dry conditions and winds gusting to 40 mph allowed the fire to advance rapidly before it was brought under control.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of fast moving thunderstorms produced the first significant severe weather event of the season.  Spotters and law enforcement officials reported wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph, which knocked down numerous trees and power lines, as well as damaged some crops.

A line of fast moving thunderstorms produced the first significant severe weather event of the season.  Spotters and law enforcement officials reported wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph, which knocked down numerous trees and power lines, as well as damaged some crops.

A line of fast moving thunderstorms produced the first significant severe weather event of the season.  Spotters and law enforcement officials reported wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph, which knocked down numerous trees and power lines, as well as damaged some crops.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including large trees down.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including 2 to 4 inch diameter branches down.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including 3 inch diameter branches blown down. The wind also blew shingles off a house.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including a large tree down onto a building.
Trees and power lines were blown down.
A 40x72 foot storage barn was completely demolished, with debris spread downwind several hundred feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain affected much of southeast Minnesota, with ice accumulations of 1/8 to 1/4 inch.  In a few locations, such as Lake City and Zumbro Falls (Wabasha County), the ice was as much as 1/2 inch thick.  Law enforcement officials reported numerous automobile accidents due to icy roads, while there were a few power outages.

Freezing rain affected much of southeast Minnesota, with ice accumulations of 1/8 to 1/4 inch.  In a few locations, such as Lake City and Zumbro Falls (Wabasha County), the ice was as much as 1/2 inch thick.  Law enforcement officials reported numerous automobile accidents due to icy roads, while there were a few power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees were also uprooted and many large branches were blown down.
Main street was flooded with water up to 18 inches deep.  Several businesses were threatened and they required sandbags at their front doors to keep the water out.
Flooding ditches caused some basement flooding.
Several trees were blown down and a trampoline was blown across a yard.  The area was without power for 4 hours.
A MN DOT RWIS site along U.S. Highway 10 measured the wind gust.  Tree damage was also reported at the town cemetery and several trees fell on homes in town.
The roof was torn off a turkey barn and thrown across U.S. Highway 10.
Two large trees fell on a truck near Isle of Pines on Lake Vermilion, barely missing its occupants.
Winds were also estimated at 80 mph.
There were also widespread damaging winds. Damage was mainly to trees and power lines.
Severe thunderstorms with strong winds were prevalent mainly north and east of Virginia. Two thousand Lake Country Power customers lost power.
A brief waterspout touched down on Roosevelt Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large tree fell on the tent of a couple who were camping at Jackfish Bay of Basswood Lake. The woman was killed while her 42 year old fiance was knocked unconscious.
Large trees were blown over.  The top of a tree broke off and fell through the windshield of a man's car.  A branch impaled the 58 year-old man to the car seat.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.
Large tree branches were snapped off.
The hail was reported at Lake Itasca State Park.
A few shingles were blown off roofs.  A MN DOT RWIS sensor near Badger reported a wind gust to 52 mph near the time of this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of fast moving thunderstorms produced the first significant severe weather event of the season.  Spotters and law enforcement officials reported wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph, which knocked down numerous trees and power lines, as well as damaged some crops.

A line of fast moving thunderstorms produced the first significant severe weather event of the season.  Spotters and law enforcement officials reported wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph, which knocked down numerous trees and power lines, as well as damaged some crops.

A line of fast moving thunderstorms produced the first significant severe weather event of the season.  Spotters and law enforcement officials reported wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph, which knocked down numerous trees and power lines, as well as damaged some crops.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several 5 inch diameter tree branches were snapped off by the strong wind.  A MN DOT RWIS site nearby reported a wind gust to 50 mph near the time of this event.
Several 5 inch diameter tree branches were broken off by the wind.  A MN DOT RWIS site in the area reported a 50 mph wind gust near the time of this event.
The AWOS at the Bemidji airport reported a 58 mph wind gust.
A brief waterspout was reported over Lower Red Lake.
Small 6 inch diameter trees were blown down.
Roofing material was blown off the Kelliher Cafe.  Several large trees were also blown down.
Numerous trees were blown down in West Duluth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging in size from dimes and pennies to as large as ping-pong balls was reported by law enforcement officials, amateur radio operators, storm spotters and the general public.
Hail ranging in size from dimes and pennies to as large as ping-pong balls was reported by law enforcement officials, amateur radio operators, storm spotters and the general public.
